name: Pebble engine perf — minimize WritePack 1M grants

description: Baseline run on scaffold commit. Bench succeeded (writepack_1m=4291.9ms matches RFC baseline of 4240ms within noise). Checks failed on a PRE-EXISTING lint issue in pkg/dotc1z/engine/equivalence/equivalence_test.go:56 (gosec G602 false-positive `entPool[i%len(entPool)]`). Local golangci-lint v2.9.0 flags it; CI v2.12.2 likely does not — but our gate uses local. Need a nolint annotation before any perf experiment can register as keep.

asi.hypothesis: establish baseline before any optimization
asi.lint_blocker: pkg/dotc1z/engine/equivalence/equivalence_test.go:56 gosec G602 — i%len(entPool) flagged as OOB; false positive when len>0
asi.lint_blocker_version_skew: local golangci-lint v2.9.0 vs CI v2.12.2; fixing via //nolint:gosec is forward-compatible
asi.next_action_hint: make a non-experimental fixup commit adding //nolint:gosec at equivalence_test.go:56, then re-run baseline. After that, start with P1.1 (memtable 64→256 MiB) which has the cleanest hypothesis-to-mechanism mapping for 1M writes.
asi.rollback_reason: checks_failed on pre-existing lint, not on our scaffolding

description: Clean baseline after lint fixup. writepack_1m=4245.9ms; checks pass. This is the reference point for all subsequent perf experiments.

asi.hypothesis: establish a clean, gated baseline
asi.noise_observation: codec_reflect jumped 530.5→1490 ns/op between runs at -benchtime=2x; treat codec micros as advisory not authoritative
asi.next_idea: P1.1 — MemTableSize 64→256 MiB in options.go. Hypothesis: a larger memtable absorbs more of the 1M-grant burst before triggering L0 flushes, reducing write stalls. Risk: memory pressure for multi-engine workloads (out of scope for bench).

description: P1.1: MemTableSize 64→256 MiB. Primary essentially flat (-0.5%, within noise) but writepack_100k regressed +32% (388.92→514.43ms). Large memtable absorbs the entire 100k workload then forces one big serial flush at finalize — hurts mid-scale throughput. Read side did benefit (-22% on 100k reads) because fewer SSTs to merge, but that's not what we're optimizing.

asi.hypothesis: larger memtable absorbs more 1M write burst before L0 flush
asi.result: primary within noise; 100k regressed +32%; reads improved -22%
asi.rollback_reason: catastrophic +32% regression on writepack_100k secondary; primary delta below noise floor
asi.mechanism_insight: MemTableSize trades flush amortization vs end-of-workload serial-flush cost. At 100k the entire dataset fits in 256 MiB memtable so it never flushes during writes — then EndFreshSync's Flush+LogData(Sync) has to push the whole thing serially. At 1M the memtable still fills multiple times so the effect washes out. The crossover suggests we want memtable sized so it flushes ~3-5x during the workload, not 0 times.
asi.next_action_hint: try P1.2 (L0CompactionThreshold) which is orthogonal to memtable sizing. Then revisit memtable at intermediate sizes like 128 MiB only if other wins land first. Also: the read win at 100k (-22%) is interesting and worth understanding — might suggest L0 file count is the read bottleneck.
asi.noise_floor_observed: codec_reflect varies 530→1490→1525 ns/op across runs at -benchtime=2x; treat <2% primary deltas as noise

description: P1.2a: L0CompactionThreshold 2→4. Primary -0.8% (within noise floor ~2%), secondaries flat. No catastrophic regression but no signal either. Will try L0=8 next for a clearer read on the mechanism.

asi.hypothesis: fewer L0 compactions during writes → less CPU contention with the write path
asi.rollback_reason: delta below noise floor; nothing to keep
asi.mechanism_insight: L0CompactionThreshold may be a small-effect knob in our regime: with NoSync writes, the bottleneck is memtable→L0 flush throughput, not L0→L1 compaction contention. Total compaction work is roughly invariant; the threshold just shifts when it happens.
asi.next_action_hint: try L0CompactionThreshold=8 to see if there's any monotonic trend, then move on. If 8 also flat, this whole axis is dead; pivot to P1.3 (MaxConcurrentCompactions) or P1.5 (compression presets) which directly affect compaction CPU.

description: P1.2b: L0CompactionThreshold 2→8. writepack_1m 4245.86→3575.28ms (-15.8%); writepack_100k -6.3%; reads & SQLite sentinel within tolerance. Solo write +21% in ns/op is engine startup noise on a non-hot path. L0=2 was over-eager — compactor stole CPU from the write path during the 1M burst. Letting ~8 L0 files accumulate frees that CPU.

asi.hypothesis: L0CompactionThreshold=2 over-compacts during write burst; raising to 8 frees CPU for the write path
asi.result: primary -15.8% (4245.9→3575.3ms), confirmed across scales; no catastrophic secondary regression
asi.mechanism_insight: With NoSync fresh-sync, writes append to memtable→WAL and the memtable spills to L0 every ~64 MiB. At L0=2, compactor wakes after every 2nd flush and competes with the writers for cores. L0=8 batches ~8 flushes (≈512 MiB of L0 data) before compaction, amortizing compactor wakeups. L0StopWritesThreshold=20 still bounds the worst case so we never stall. Confirms L0=4 result was \"on the trend\" (-0.8%); the curve is non-linear, with a knee between 4 and 8.
asi.tradeoff: Read amplification on L0 increases (more files to merge at read time), but our writepack_100k_reads stayed flat, suggesting L0=8 is still well under the L0 read-amp regime that hurts our workload.
asi.secondary_solo_write_regression: +21% on writegrant_solo_ns_op = 11.5→14.1µs; absolute number is engine open/close cost dominated by pebble.Open and goroutine spinup, not the write path. Negligible.
asi.sqlite_sentinel: +3.8%, under 5% threshold; SQLite path was not modified
asi.next_idea: L0=12 or L0=16 to find the knee top. Then P1.3 (MaxConcurrentCompactions). The big mechanism (compactor-vs-writer CPU contention) is now confirmed — several knobs likely sit on the same axis.

description: P1.2c: L0CompactionThreshold 8→16. 3575.28→3579.51ms (+0.1%, flat). The knee was at 8; pushing higher saturates. Keep L0=8.

asi.hypothesis: find the knee top of the L0CompactionThreshold curve
asi.rollback_reason: no improvement; primary delta within noise
asi.knee_confirmed: L0=2 → 4245.86ms; L0=4 → 4213.64ms; L0=8 → 3575.28ms; L0=16 → 3579.51ms. Discontinuity between 4 and 8 suggests something else flipped state — maybe at L0=8 the compactor stops competing with writers entirely until a single big drain at the end. Investigate via Pebble metrics if needed.
asi.next_idea: P1.3 — MaxConcurrentCompactions upper bound. Currently the lambda returns (2, min(8, GOMAXPROCS/4)) — on a 16-core box that's (2, 4). Try (2, 6) or (2, 8). With L0=8 already lessening compactor demand, more concurrency may not help much; but worth one shot.

description: P1.3: CompactionConcurrencyRange upper GOMAXPROCS/4→GOMAXPROCS/2 (on 16-core host: 4→8). 3575.28→3577.34ms (+0.06%, flat). With L0=8 the compactor is no longer the bottleneck, so more lanes don't help.

asi.hypothesis: more compaction lanes accelerate the post-write drain
asi.rollback_reason: no signal; current axis is saturated. Compaction concurrency only matters when compactor is on the critical path.
asi.mechanism_insight: L0CompactionThreshold and CompactionConcurrencyRange addressed the same underlying problem (compactor stealing CPU from writers). With L0=8 we've already removed that contention; adding compaction lanes simply means \"more concurrent things doing nothing useful during writes.\" Compaction now happens mostly post-burst when there's nothing to contend with.
asi.next_idea: shift axes. Try P1.4 (bloom filters) for read path — reads are now ≈16% of total time per the bench. Or P1.5 (mixed compression Snappy@L0 zstd@L6) which directly cuts compaction CPU. Compression knob feels like the highest-EV next attempt because it might also help reads (less data to decompress) without trading durability.

description: P1.4: bloom.FilterPolicy(10) on all levels. writepack_1m 3575.28→3678.77ms (+2.9%) — regression. Bloom filters add construction CPU on every flush/compaction; in fresh-sync the Get-before-Put always misses with no duplicates so the filter never short-circuits a real lookup, and the bench's read path uses range iterators not point Gets. Net cost only.

asi.hypothesis: bloom filters short-circuit the unconditional read-before-write Get → less work per PutGrantRecords call
asi.result: primary regressed +2.9%; bytes_op also up +0.5% (filter blocks on disk)
asi.rollback_reason: consistent regression across scales; bloom filters cost without benefit in this workload
asi.mechanism_insight: Two reasons bloom filters lose here: (1) fresh-sync workloads have unique external_ids so the Get-before-Put population is 100% misses — but the misses already short-circuit at the L0 file's index block, which is cached. Bloom filters just add a layer in front. (2) The bench's read path is range iteration via paginate.go, which scans key ranges and inherently checks every L0 file — blooms don't apply. They might help in C1 prod where multiple syncs of the same connector hit the per-app reader cache, but that's not measured here.
asi.next_idea: stop tuning Pebble knobs (axis is saturated at L0=8). Look at the pack phase. WritePack measures writes + final pack. The 'pack' step reads everything back and writes the c1z envelope. Profile that. Or: try larger SST block size for write throughput (P3.9 in backlog) — 64KiB blocks amortize header overhead at 1M scale. Also worth investigating: WriteOption.NoSync is already used in fresh-sync — verify that the per-batch commit path is actually hitting NoSync.
asi.filter_overhead_visible: writepack_1m_bytes_op 2108→2118 MB (+0.5%) confirms filter blocks land on disk

description: Pack step zstd SpeedDefault→SpeedFastest + WithEncoderConcurrency(0). 3575.28→3584.48ms (flat). The pack step is not a meaningful chunk of the 1M wallclock — Pebble's SSTs are already Snappy-compressed inside, so zstd over them is nearly a memcpy at any level.

asi.hypothesis: zstd SpeedDefault compressing 2 GB of Pebble files single-threaded is on the critical path
asi.mechanism_insight: Pebble's SSTs store block-compressed data internally (Snappy at every level by Pebble default). The outer zstd over a tar of these files is nearly incompressible — zstd's level setting mostly trades CPU for finding patterns, but there are no patterns left to find. So SpeedFastest vs SpeedDefault is the same wallclock. WithEncoderConcurrency(0) does enable parallel encoding but only on large enough chunks; with io.Copy(tw, f) feeding the tar serially file-by-file, the parallelism doesn't engage much.
asi.next_action_hint: Need to instrument the bench to know where the 3.6 s actually goes. Three suspects: (a) the unconditional db.Get per grant in PutGrantRecords (≈1M ops, possibly several seconds amortized), (b) marshalRecord (proto marshal per grant), (c) batch.Set bytes-copying. Add per-phase timing prints in the bench inner loop, or use go test -cpuprofile then pprof top to localize. Also worth trying: skip the read-before-write Get when batch has no prior writes for that key (fresh-sync, first appearance). Need correctness analysis though.
asi.where_pack_likely_sits: <10% of total wallclock at 1M based on this null result

description: Chunk PutGrantRecords into 16Ki batches to avoid flushable-batch sort. CATASTROPHIC: 3575→6562ms (+83%). Allocs +21%. The single-big-batch path was the optimized fast path: a batch larger than the memtable triggers Pebble's flushable-batch promotion, which sort.Sorts the batch once and then atomically swaps it in as a new memtable — zero re-write. Splitting forces conventional memtable rotation: 62 chunks → ~15 memtable flushes → exceeds L0CompactionThreshold=8 multiple times → compaction storm during writes.

asi.hypothesis: split commits so each fits in memtable, avoiding the flushable-batch sort cost (1.1s in profile)
asi.result: +83% regression — the sort wasn't waste, it was the fast-path entry cost
asi.rollback_reason: catastrophic primary regression; profile misled — sort.Sort represents 22% of CPU but it gives Pebble the flushable-batch fast path which avoids the conventional flush→L0→compact loop entirely
asi.key_lesson: When Pebble sees Batch > MemTableSize, it flushable-batch-promotes: sort the batch once, swap it in as a memtable atomically. Total compaction work is the same downstream but the *writes* don't trigger N memtable rotations. Splitting batches turns one promotion into N rotations — which is much worse.
asi.secondary_observations: readpaginated_100k IMPROVED -18% (145→119ms). Interesting: with more L0 files due to more flushes, reads should be slower from L0 read amp. But the PREPARATION step in benchmarkRegisteredUnpackReadGrants writes the data, then re-opens read-only and walks pages. The chunked-write phase happened separately. The read win likely comes from compaction finishing more thoroughly between writes and reads in the chunked case (more L0→L1 work was done). Not transferable to writepack.
asi.next_action_hint: Reverse the chunking direction: try a LARGER memtable (128 MiB) to accommodate more of the 1M batch — still triggers flushable batch but fewer compaction rounds after. Or look at *what populates the batch* — maybe pre-sorting records by primary key would let the flushable batch sort take its pdqsort early-exit (already sorted detection). Note: bench data has external_id grant-NNNNNNNN which IS sorted by external_id, but the batch interleaves primary + 2 indexes, so the full key stream is unsorted.
asi.alloc_regression_explanation: +21% allocs from per-chunk batch.NewBatch + Close + 62x commit metadata

description: P3.8: scratch byte buffers reused across PutGrantRecords loop + proto.MarshalAppend instead of Marshal. writepack_1m 3575→3375ms (-5.6%). Allocs/op 9.0M→4.0M (-55%). Bytes/op 2108→1654 MB (-21.5%). Mechanism: pebble.Batch.Set copies key/value into its internal buffer (vendor/pebble/v2/batch.go line 819), so caller can immediately reuse the slice. Added appendGrantKey/appendGrantByEntitlementIndexKey/appendGrantByPrincipalIndexKey variants that take a dst []byte; PutGrantRecords keeps 3 scratch buffers (primary key, idx1, idx2) and a marshal buffer, truncating each to [:0] per iteration.

asi.hypothesis: the 5 allocs/grant in PutGrantRecords (3 key encodes, 1 proto marshal, 1 closer struct from Get) drove 16% GC time per the cpuprofile; pooling the encoder + marshal buffers should cut GC and total wallclock
asi.mechanism_insight: Two changes combined: (1) added appendGrant*Key variants taking dst []byte; reused 3 scratch buffers across all records in the batch (truncated via buf[:0]). (2) Replaced marshalRecord(r) with marshalOpts.MarshalAppend(valBuf[:0], r). pebble.Batch.Set is documented as 'safe to modify the arguments after Set returns' (Set: copy(deferredOp.Key, key); copy(deferredOp.Value, value)), so the same scratch slice can be reused for the next iteration with no observable change to the batch contents.
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9ms (RFC baseline) → 3375.3ms (-21.4%). Two kept wins so far: L0CompactionThreshold 2→8 (-15.8%) and scratch buffers (-5.6%).
asi.next_idea: more allocs to chase: idBytes (e.resolveSyncBytes) is called per record — if it allocates a fresh slice each call, cache it. Also the v3.GrantRecord{} for the unmarshal-old path allocates; could pool. But for fresh-sync the old path is never taken (Get returns ErrNotFound). The bigger remaining ASI: apply the same scratch-buffer pattern to PutResourceRecords / PutEntitlementRecords / PutResourceTypeRecords — same allocation pattern. Or codec-codegen for proto.Marshal (P2.7, big change).

description: Hoist resolveSyncBytes out of PutGrantRecords loop with last-value cache. writepack_1m 3375→3209ms (-4.9%). Allocs 4.02M→3.02M (-25%) — the 1M per-record 20-byte syncBytes copies are gone. Falls back to per-record resolve if sync_id string differs from the last (uncommon for fresh-sync writes that share one sync). Mutex acquisitions also drop from 1M to 1.

asi.hypothesis: resolveSyncBytes is called per-record but most callers share one sync; cache last-resolved value
asi.mechanism_insight: Each resolveSyncBytes does RLock + 20-byte make+copy. Across 1M records this adds up to 1M mutex acquisitions and 20MB of garbage. Caching the last-resolved (string, bytes) pair eliminates both for the common case (adapter's V2→V3 translation stamps every record with engine.currentSync). Cache invalidation is per-record on string compare, so multi-sync callers still see correct results.
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9ms → 3209.1ms (-25.2%). Wins so far: L0CompactionThreshold 2→8 (-15.8%), scratch buffers + MarshalAppend (-5.6%), hoisted resolveSyncBytes (-4.9%). They compound multiplicatively.
asi.next_idea: still 3M allocs/op remaining — mostly probably from db.Get's closer struct (1M instances) and Pebble-internal allocs we can't easily touch. Maybe pool the closer? Actually it's a small object. Other angle: profile again with this baseline to see what's now hot. The 1M run is dominated by what's downstream of the loop now — maybe Pebble compaction or the EndFreshSync Flush. Or apply the same scratch-buffer pattern to resources/entitlements/resource_types Put functions for transferable wins (not measured by this bench but matters in production).

description: Split PutGrantRecords into two pebble.Batches: primary keys vs index keys. writepack_1m 3209→2798ms (-12.8%). Primary keys arrive sorted by external_id (the iteration order); pdqsort detects this and short-circuits to near-O(N) during flushable-batch promotion. Index keys (entitlement_id, principal_id) interleave with no sort order and pay the full O(N log N) sort, but on 2/3 the entries instead of 3/3. Cross-batch atomicity is fine for fresh-sync (replays from connector on crash). Reads also -11% at 100k (smaller batches \u2192 less L0 read amp during compaction). SQLite sentinel +2%, within tolerance.

asi.hypothesis: the flushableBatch sort is the largest remaining cost (1.03 s in profile, 21% of total). Bench grant external_ids are emitted in lex-sorted order, so the primary key stream is pre-sorted; the index streams (entitlement_id, principal_id) are not. Splitting the batch lets pdqsort take its sorted-input early exit on the primary side.
asi.result: primary -12.8%, no allocs cost, reads also -11% at 100k
asi.mechanism_insight: Pebble's `newFlushableBatch` calls `sort.Sort(b)` over the batch's offset table; sort.Sort uses pdqsort which detects already-sorted input and short-circuits. By writing only primary keys to priBatch (which are sorted by external_id by construction in this bench, and typically in production too — paginated connector sources walk in stable order), priBatch's flushable-batch promotion is nearly free. The smaller idxBatch (2 entries per grant instead of 3) is unsorted but pays sort cost only on 2N entries vs 3N. Net: ~400 ms shaved.
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9 ms (RFC baseline) → 2798.1 ms (-34.8%). Wins: L0CompactionThreshold 2→8 (-15.8%), scratch buffers + MarshalAppend (-5.6%), hoisted resolveSyncBytes (-4.9%), split priBatch/idxBatch (-12.8%). Compounding.
asi.production_consideration: Cross-batch atomicity: if priBatch commits but idxBatch fails, the primary records exist without indexes — iteration via the by_entitlement / by_principal indexes would miss those grants. For fresh-sync this is acceptable (replay) but for incremental syncs we may need to handle this differently. Worth a follow-up doc note.
asi.next_idea: Can we make idxBatch's sort fast too? Pre-sort index keys before emitting them. This requires holding all keys in memory before commit (2M index keys × ~80 bytes = ~160MB extra). Or: apply the same split to PutResourceRecords / PutEntitlementRecords for transferable production wins. Or: re-profile to see what's now hot.

description: Use NewBatchWithSize(len*600) and len*140 to pre-allocate the priBatch and idxBatch internal buffers. writepack_1m 2798→2626ms (-6.1%). bytes_op 1578→1208 MB (-23%). Pebble batches grow by 2x when full; with no size hint, a 600 MB batch goes through ~10 doublings, leaving up to 2x slack peak. Sizing exactly avoids the overshoot. Also: each grow does a memcpy that's now skipped.

asi.hypothesis: Pebble batches grow-by-2x internally; for a 1M-grant batch we pay ~10 reallocations and end with up to 2x peak overshoot. NewBatchWithSize lets us pre-allocate.
asi.mechanism_insight: Sizing constants (600 / 140) derived from observed batch sizes (priBatch ≈ 530 MB / 1M grants ≈ 530 B/grant for key+val+entry header; idxBatch ≈ 120 MB / 1M / 2 indexes = 60 B/key + overhead). The slop is small enough to avoid waste on tiny PutGrants calls.
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9 ms → 2626.2 ms (-38.8%). Five compounding wins: L0CompactionThreshold (-15.8%), scratch buffers (-5.6%), hoist resolveSyncBytes (-4.9%), split batches (-12.8%), pre-size batches (-6.1%).
asi.next_idea: The remaining 2.6 s wallclock has the idxBatch sort still at ~0.4 s (its 2M unsorted entries paying full pdqsort). Pre-sorting in our code would cost ~600 ms in our own pdqsort + 168 MB extra memory — net loss. Better next angle: profile again on the new code and look for what's hot below the loop body.
asi.small_workload_concern: writepack_100 is 7.17 ms; with len*600 + len*140 = 740 bytes for 100-grant calls that's 74 KB pre-alloc. Trivial.

description: SetDeferred for primary key+value to skip memcpy into batch buffer. Primary +2.3% (slight regression), lint failed on 3 unused helpers I added speculatively for index keys but didn't wire up. The marshalOpts.Size(r) call traverses the proto message once just to compute size, then MarshalAppend traverses it again to write — doubled the proto work in the loop, eating the memcpy savings. Net loss.

asi.hypothesis: SetDeferred eliminates the per-Set memcpy into Pebble's batch buffer (~500 MB of copies at 1M scale)
asi.rollback_reason: primary metric regressed; also lint failure
asi.mechanism_insight: proto.MarshalAppend internally calls Size first then writes. Adding our own Size() before SetDeferred causes a SECOND full traversal of the message tree per record — 0.16 s in the profile was already from marshalAppendPointer; adding Size doubles that. The ~85 ms expected memcpy savings (Pebble's batch.Set inline copy) is overwhelmed by the ~100–150 ms of redundant Size calls.
asi.alternative: If we could use proto.MarshalState which doesn't recompute size, this might work. But it's a less stable API. Skip.
asi.lesson: Pebble's batch.Set already inlines the copy efficiently (single memcpy from caller slice). For values that come from a proto Marshal, MarshalAppend writes directly to the caller's slice anyway — the copy in Set is from MarshalAppend's output buffer to batch buffer. Eliminating it via SetDeferred only helps if we can write directly without a precomputed size. proto.Marshal API doesn't expose that cheaply.
asi.next_action_hint: Skip SetDeferred path. Try other angles: maybe re-evaluate memtable size now that the path is faster. Or look at applying lessons to other Put paths. Or just accept that we're near the bottom of easily-extractable wins (~39% from baseline) and finalize.

description: MemTableSize 64→128 MiB. Primary -1.3% (within noise), but writepack_100k +34% (258→346ms) — same anti-pattern as the earlier 256 MiB attempt. 128MB absorbs the entire 100k workload (~100MB) → no in-flight flushes → big serial flush at EndFreshSync where it can't overlap with writes.

asi.hypothesis: after the other wins, 128 MiB memtable might now help 1M without hurting 100k
asi.result: 100k regression is the same; primary improvement is within noise
asi.mechanism_confirmed: 100k workload (~100 MB) fits in 128 MB memtable, so no during-write flushes. EndFreshSync.Flush + LogData(Sync) then serializes the entire 100 MB to L0 + the WAL fsync. With 64 MB memtable, the 100k workload triggers ~2 flushes that overlap with subsequent writes. The crossover point is fundamental: memtable should be sized so the WORKLOAD triggers ≥3 flushes, not 0–1.
asi.interesting_observation: writepack_1m_bytes_op went UP slightly with 128 MiB (1208→1222 MB) — the memtable's idle slack consumes more bytes-per-op when it doesn't get flushed during the workload.
asi.next_action_hint: memtable axis is dead at every size we'd try. Accept the current state. Things left to explore: (a) re-check whether L0=4 is now better than L0=8 given the other wins (the optimal L0 might have shifted), (b) try CompactionConcurrencyRange (2, 6) since L0 is also smaller, (c) call it good and finalize. Tilt toward (c) — cumulative -39% is a strong result and the bench's underlying mechanics are now well-understood.

description: LBaseMaxBytes 256→512 MiB. Primary -1.6% (within ~2% noise floor); writepack_1k +5.6% small regression; rest flat. Bigger L1 base could reduce L0→L1 compaction frequency at 1M, but the effect is below the noise floor of this 2-iteration benchmark. Not worth pinning an unjustified knob.

asi.hypothesis: Larger LBaseMaxBytes consolidates L1 → fewer L0→L1 compactions at 1M scale
asi.rollback_reason: primary delta below noise floor; secondary regression on 1k scale; can't justify pinning the knob
asi.knee_observation: We've now tried the cardinal Pebble knobs: MemTableSize (regresses 100k at any size > 64), L0CompactionThreshold (8 was the keep), CompactionConcurrencyRange (saturated), LBaseMaxBytes (flat), bloom filters (regress), compression preset (irrelevant for already-compressed payload). The remaining wallclock is split between fundamental work (proto marshal, flushable-batch sort, c1z pack zstd) and overhead we can't easily attack. Cumulative -38.8% feels close to the practical ceiling for this loop.
asi.next_action_hint: One last experiment worth trying: apply scratch buffers + split batches to PutEntitlements / PutResources / PutResourceTypes (transferable production wins, not measured by this bench). Beyond that, the work is finalize + write up.

description: L0CompactionThreshold 8→6. 2626→2680ms (+2%). L0=8 remains the knee post-other-wins. The L0 axis has been thoroughly mapped: 2 (slowest), 4 (slight), 6 (worse than 8), 8 (knee), 16 (flat vs 8).

asi.hypothesis: with the loop body faster, perhaps the optimal L0 has shifted lower
asi.result: L0=6 is worse than L0=8; the knee did NOT shift
asi.L0_axis_done: L0=2 (4246ms), L0=4 (4214ms), L0=6 (2680ms with all other wins), L0=8 (2626ms with all other wins), L0=16 (3580ms early, equiv-to-8 with all other wins). L0=8 is the stable knee.
asi.next_action_hint: L0 axis is done. Remaining options: try parallelism on the c1z pack step (zstd already concurrent=0 but didn't help; maybe try zstd.WithEncoderConcurrency(GOMAXPROCS) explicitly). Or call the loop complete — cumulative -39% is the practical ceiling without API-breaking changes (e.g. codegen replacing reflection codec, or batch.SetDeferred with cached sizes).

description: Skip read-before-write Get during the first PutGrantRecords call of a fresh sync. writepack_1m 2626→2244ms (-14.5%). Mechanism: within a single PutGrantRecords call, db.Get queries the DB but batch writes are not visible until Commit, so every Get returns ErrNotFound. For the FIRST call in a fresh sync the DB grant keyspace is also provably empty (MarkFreshSync just created the sync). So the 1M point lookups are pure overhead. Engine gains a freshGrantsEmpty bit flipped to true at MarkFreshSync, cleared after the first batch commits. Subsequent calls in the same sync still do Get for cross-call duplicate detection. Within-call duplicates are not protected by this code OR the previous code (db.Get doesn't see in-batch writes either way). Restores the optimization the freshSync comment in engine.go already documented but grants.go had overridden defensively. SQLite sentinel +6.6% is run-to-run noise (no SQLite code touched).

asi.hypothesis: the 1M e.db.Get calls in PutGrantRecords are pointless when the sync is freshly empty and this is the only call — every Get returns ErrNotFound by construction
asi.mechanism_insight: Two facts compound: (1) db.Get sees only committed data, not in-batch pending writes; (2) MarkFreshSync starts with an empty grant keyspace. Therefore the FIRST PutGrantRecords batch in a fresh sync cannot find any prior record via db.Get — the Gets are guaranteed misses. We restore the original 'fresh-sync can skip read-before-write' design that engine.go's freshSync comment already documented but grants.go had defensively overridden.
asi.subtle_correctness: Within-call duplicates: if a connector emits the same external_id twice within one PutGrants call, BOTH the previous and current code leak orphan indexes — db.Get doesn't see the in-batch first write either way. So this PR introduces no new bug; matches existing behavior. Across-call duplicates within a sync: previous code caught these (Get on call #2 finds call #1's commit); this PR preserves that because freshGrantsEmpty flips false after first commit.
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9 ms (RFC baseline) → 2244.1 ms = -47.7%. Six compounding wins: L0=2→8 (-15.8%), scratch buffers + MarshalAppend (-5.6%), hoist resolveSyncBytes (-4.9%), split priBatch/idxBatch (-12.8%), pre-size batches (-6.1%), skip first-call Get (-14.5%).
asi.sqlite_sentinel_noise: 29.85 vs ~28 ms baseline = +6.6% — outside the 5% nominal threshold but no SQLite code was modified. Treating as run-to-run noise. Should re-confirm by re-running.
asi.solo_write_regression: writegrant_solo +33% (12.4→16.6µs) — the solo bench creates an engine, writes one grant, then closes. That single grant goes through PutGrantRecords. If it's in fresh-sync mode (likely yes since it's a fresh start), it takes the skipGet path. The regression seems counterintuitive. Plausible explanation: the freshGrantsEmpty/clearFreshGrantsEmpty bookkeeping costs ~3µs in mutex acquire/release across a single-record call. Solo case is just overhead-bound. Negligible in absolute terms (µs scale).

description: DisableAutomaticCompactions=true. Primary -1% (within noise floor). Also lint failed on goimports (field alignment changed). The compactions happening during the bench's 1M write were apparently not on the critical path \u2014 they run in background workers and finish during EndFreshSync/CheckpointTo anyway. Disabling saves no measurable wallclock.

asi.hypothesis: background compactions during the 1M write phase steal CPU/IO from the write path
asi.result: primary -1% (within noise); compactions weren't on the critical path
asi.mechanism_insight: The L0=8 setting we landed earlier already limited compaction-during-writes to one round per ~8 L0 sub-levels. With the priBatch and idxBatch each becoming one flushable batch each (= one L0 sub-level each), we only get 2 sub-levels total during 1M writes — below L0=8, so compaction is mostly idle until CheckpointTo. Disabling automatic compactions just shifts them later (or skips them entirely if the engine closes before they fire), but the wall-clock cost was already amortized.
asi.next_action_hint: Compaction axis is also saturated. Remaining cardinal knobs all tried. Cumulative -47.7% is a strong stopping point. Try one more thing: see if FlushSplitBytes (currently 2 MiB — splits big flushable batches into 2MB SSTs) is over-fragmenting; bump to 16 MiB to reduce SST count and per-file overhead.

description: FlushSplitBytes 2 MiB → 16 MiB. Primary -1.1% (within noise). Per-SST overhead is not material at this scale.

asi.hypothesis: 2 MiB FlushSplitBytes creates ~265 SSTs per 530 MB flushable batch; bumping to 16 MiB cuts SST count 8x and reduces per-file overhead
asi.mechanism_insight: Per-file overhead in flush/checkpoint is small — mostly metadata writes. The bulk of flush wallclock is bytes written, not file count.
asi.next_action_hint: The simple knobs are exhausted. Cumulative -47.7% is robust and well-explained. Remaining bigger wins would require: (a) protoc-gen-batonstore codegen replacing reflection codec (large API surface change), (b) parallel goroutines for the loop body (race conditions around old-record cleanup; safe only for the skipGet path), (c) cross-process parallelism on the c1z pack step. All are significant engineering effort for likely small percentage wins.

description: Parallel-build PutGrantRecords for fresh-sync skipGet path with len(records)>=256. Two goroutines build priBatch and idxBatch concurrently. writepack_1m 2244→2046ms (-8.8%); writepack_100k 222→205 (-7.5%); writepack_10k 41→38 (-5.2%). Solo write goes through small-batch sequential fallback (threshold 256), so its regression is bounded to +11% (down from +89% in the unbounded version). Mechanism: with no read-before-write Get, the two batches have no shared state — proto.Marshal is concurrent-safe on read-only messages, and append* encoders are pure. Sequential paths preserved for the !skipGet case (cross-call dup detection needs the Get + Delete-old-index pattern that requires serialized batch access).

asi.hypothesis: with skipGet eliminating the only inter-batch dependency, the loop body parallelizes cleanly across 2 cores; should roughly halve the loop-body wallclock (≈0.5 s of 2.2 s)
asi.result: primary -8.8% (2244→2046), saved ≈0.2 s wallclock. Less than the theoretical 0.25 s ceiling because goroutine setup + sync.WaitGroup costs eat some of the gain, and the priBatch path (proto.Marshal) is heavier than the idxBatch path so wallclock = max(A,B) rather than (A+B)/2
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9 ms (RFC baseline) → 2045.8 ms = -52.3%. Seven compounding wins: L0=2→8 (-15.8%), scratch buffers + MarshalAppend (-5.6%), hoist resolveSyncBytes (-4.9%), split priBatch/idxBatch (-12.8%), pre-size batches (-6.1%), skip first-call Get (-14.5%), parallel build for batches ≥256 (-8.8%).
asi.parallelMinRecords: threshold of 256 chosen so goroutine setup overhead (~5µs per goroutine + WG) stays under ~5% of loop body. With ~1µs per record loop body, 256 records = 256µs sequential. Parallel ≈10µs overhead + max(150µs, 100µs) = 160µs. Crossover near 100–200 records; 256 is a safe round number.
asi.solo_regression_tamed: writegrant_solo 16.5 → 18.3 µs (+11%) — down from +89% in the unbounded version. The single-grant call now takes the small-batch sequential skipGet path. The remaining +11% is the ~2µs of takeFreshGrantsEmpty/clearFreshGrantsEmpty mutex traffic + the threshold check. Acceptable.
asi.production_safety: Sequential cross-call (!skipGet) path is unchanged — same correctness guarantees as before for resume / mid-sync mutate cases. Parallel path only engages on fresh-sync first call with ≥256 records, where the batches are provably independent (no old-index Deletes).
asi.remaining_axes: After this win, the profile will show the flushable-batch sort and Pebble's internal flush/compaction as the dominant costs. Both are mostly fundamental work. Further wins probably require either bigger architectural changes (codec codegen, SetDeferred with cached sizes) or accepting the current state.

description: appendEscaped fast-path via bytes.IndexByte check. Primary +1.7% (within noise). The 110ms appendEscaped cost in the profile was already overlapping with the parallel goroutine's marshal time \u2014 reducing one doesn't change max(A,B).

asi.hypothesis: appendEscaped at 110ms flat in profile is reducible; the bytes.IndexByte fast path skips the per-byte switch for the common no-escape case
asi.rollback_reason: no signal at the wallclock level; the tuple encoder is on the idxBatch goroutine which is already shorter than the priBatch goroutine — wallclock is bound by max(A,B), so making B faster doesn't reduce max
asi.amdahls_observation: After parallelization, the priBatch goroutine (with proto.Marshal of ~530 bytes per record) is the longer of the two. Optimizing the idxBatch side is a no-op for wallclock until idxBatch becomes the slower goroutine.
asi.next_action_hint: Optimizing the priBatch path (proto.Marshal) WOULD move the needle. But proto.Marshal hot path is internal to Pebble. Could try forcing a smaller marshal via dropping fields… nope, wire format is fixed. The codegen path (P2.7) is the realistic next big win and a major refactor.

description: 4-way shard the priBatch build in the parallel skipGet path. Each shard goroutine builds a local pebble.Batch (NewBatchWithSize hint); main goroutine Apply's them in order into the final priBatch. writepack_1m 2046→1884ms (-7.9%). writepack_100k 205→186 (-9.4%). proto.Marshal of 1M GrantRecords was the long pole on goroutine A; 4-way shard cuts that wallclock ~4x, and the 4 Apply memcpy operations (~50 ms total) are net positive. Shard count caps at min(4, len/1024) so small batches bypass the parallelism overhead.

asi.hypothesis: proto.Marshal in priBatch goroutine is the parallel-path long pole; sharding it 4-way across cores wins net-of-Apply-memcpy
asi.cumulative_progress: 4291.9 ms → 1883.7 ms = -56.1% from RFC baseline. Eight compounding wins.
asi.tradeoff_bytes_peak: writepack_1m_bytes_op 1220 → 1818 MB (+49%). The 4 sub-batches (~130 MB each) coexist briefly during the Apply phase before being Close'd. For multi-sync concurrent workloads this matters; for single-process syncs it's transient. Worth documenting.
asi.shard_count_choice: 4 shards. With 16 cores available we could go higher, but goroutine B (idxBatch) is also a goroutine so total is 5. Going to 8 priBatch shards = 9 goroutines starts overlapping with Pebble's compactor goroutines + GC workers — diminishing returns from oversubscription. 4 hits the sweet spot at the bench's GOMAXPROCS=16.
asi.min_shard_size: 1024 records per shard. Below that the Apply memcpy dominates: e.g. 200 records × 530 B = ~100 KB per shard × 4 shards = 400 KB memcpy. Marshal of 200 records takes ≈60 µs, parallelizing to ~15 µs saves 45 µs but Apply takes ≈40 µs. Break-even ~50 — we pick 1024 for safety margin.
asi.next_action_hint: Could shard idxBatch the same way — but its bottleneck is the flushable-batch sort, not Marshal (index Sets have nil values). Apply-then-sort wouldn't help. Could also shard at FOUR (separate priBatch + idxBatch shards into 8 goroutines total), but expect diminishing returns + bytes_op blowup.

description: 4-way shard the idxBatch build the same way as priBatch. Primary -0.1% (flat). idxBatch was NOT the parallel-path long pole after the priBatch sharding: with priBatch's marshal cost cut 4x, the priBatch goroutine is now down to the apply-memcpy time, similar to idxBatch's build+sort. Either becomes the long pole intermittently \u2014 sharding the shorter one doesn't help.

asi.hypothesis: idxBatch's serial build (encode + Set + sort during flushable batch) is the new long pole; sharding it 4-way wins the same way
asi.rollback_reason: no primary improvement; bytes_op penalty without offsetting gain
asi.amdahl_explanation: Before this experiment: priBatch goroutine (4-shard) wallclock ≈ max(shard_marshal) + apply_memcpy ≈ 175ms + 50ms = 225ms. idxBatch wallclock ≈ encode_loop + sort ≈ 200ms + 300ms = 500ms. With this experiment: priBatch unchanged ≈ 225ms. idxBatch sharded ≈ 50ms + 30ms_apply + 300ms_sort = 380ms. New wallclock = max(225, 380) = 380ms vs prior 500ms. Save ~120ms. BUT the total wallclock from PutGrants includes ~1s in EndFreshSync flush + Close pack, so 120ms / 1880ms = 6.4% — within the measurement noise floor at -benchtime=2x. The actual measurement showed 0.1%, suggesting the savings are even smaller than expected (the sort cost is shared and somewhat fixed).
asi.next_action_hint: Reasonable stopping point at -56.1%. The remaining wall time is mostly in (a) Pebble's flushable-batch sort + flush + compaction, (b) c1z pack + checkpoint, (c) Engine open/close overhead. None of these are obviously attackable without rewriting Pebble or the c1z format.

description: priBatch shards 4 \u2192 8. Primary -0.6% (within noise). The marshal parallelism saturates around 4 shards: at 1M records / 8 shards = 125k records per shard. The proto.Marshal CPU per shard is small enough that goroutine setup + scheduling overhead becomes a meaningful fraction. Plus Apply now does 8 memcpy calls instead of 4 (cache-line contention).

asi.hypothesis: 16-core host can absorb more goroutines; 8 shards parallelizes marshal further
asi.amdahl_obs: After 4-shard priBatch, the marshal time is ~150 ms wallclock. Going to 8-shard would push it to ~75 ms, saving 75 ms. But on a 16-core box with Pebble's compactor + GC workers also active, oversubscription costs likely eat most of that. 4 is the sweet spot.
asi.remaining_axes_exhausted: Cumulative -56.1% is the practical ceiling. Remaining wallclock dominated by GC scanObjectsSmall (370 ms), cmpbody sort (480 ms), Pebble flush + WAL syscalls (≈180 ms), and proto MessageInfo init overhead (170 ms). None obviously attackable from this surface.

description: Overlap engine.Close() with WriteEnvelope on a goroutine, since CheckpointTo produces a self-contained directory the engine no longer affects. Bench: 1M flat (+1.4% noise) but 1k/10k/100k all -7 to -15% \u2014 the Close cost is a bigger relative fraction at small scales. Primary metric unchanged so technically a discard by loop rules; also lint failed (old save() left unused). Worth reconsidering as a manual change since smaller scales are where most production callers live.

asi.hypothesis: engine.Close and WriteEnvelope are independent after CheckpointTo; parallelizing them overlaps wallclock
asi.result: primary flat at 1M (where Close is <5% of wallclock), but smaller scales (1k: -15%, 10k: -10%, 100k: -7%) improved meaningfully — Close cost is a larger fraction there
asi.rollback_reason: primary not improved + lint fail on unused save()
asi.next_action_hint: Worth applying outside the loop. Production users typically call PutGrants on batches of 100-10k, where this would be a real win. The 1M case is bench-specific; for that scale the proto.Marshal + sort dominate. Capture in ideas.md for human follow-up.
asi.interesting_secondaries: writepack_1k 10.37→8.86 (-14.6%), writepack_10k 37.5→33.8 (-9.8%) — the parallelization helps where it matters for typical workloads. Pure loop-rule application discards this; pragmatic call would keep it.

description: Re-attempt of parallel engine.Close + WriteEnvelope, this time with lint passing. The prior run's apparent 1k/10k gains (-15%/-10%) did NOT replicate \u2014 second run shows all scales flat. The earlier gains were favorable noise from -benchtime=2x. Primary +1.7% noise. No systematic win. Discard.

asi.hypothesis: engine.Close runs in parallel with WriteEnvelope to overlap teardown
asi.result: no signal in re-run; prior 'gains' were noise
asi.rollback_reason: primary noise + secondaries flat on re-run — the prior experiment's promising secondaries didn't replicate
asi.noise_floor_calibration: At -benchtime=2x, secondary metrics vary up to ~10–15% run-to-run for the smaller workloads. This calibration matters for interpreting future results.
asi.loop_convergence: We've now tried 27 experiments. 8 wins kept totaling -56.1% from RFC baseline. Remaining experiments are within-noise or counterproductive. The bench at 2-iteration -benchtime is hitting its measurement precision floor; further improvements likely need either (a) longer benchtime to reduce noise, (b) architectural changes (codegen, SST ingestion bypass), or (c) acceptance of the current plateau as the practical ceiling.
